affected_groups: Roma and disadvantaged students in Church-maintained schools
students at reformed public schools who may face displaced selective demand
content.decision_question: How far should the state extend anti-segregation admissions and zoning rules into the domain of church-maintained and private school autonomy, given existing legal protections for maintainer discretion?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can document the existence and scale of the exemption gap and prior judicial precedent, and can trace how reforms elsewhere handled similar carve-outs, but it cannot resolve the normative question of how much deference religious/institutional autonomy should receive relative to anti-discrimination goals — this is a legal-value tension evidence can inform but not decide.
content.tension: Church-maintained and private schools sit outside Klebelsberg Centre authority and retain legal exemptions (including a Curia ruling permitting segregated schooling), so any admissions reform (T3), track-age reform (T2), or comprehensive pilot (T6) risks leaving a segment of the segregation-producing sector untouched or even absorbing displaced selective demand; addressing this via T4's legal exemption review directly confronts institutional and religious-autonomy protections.
content.title: Institutional autonomy of church/private maintainers versus uniform anti-segregation coverage
value_poles: religious/institutional autonomy and pluralism in school maintenance
universal, non-discriminatory access and anti-segregation coverage
